programs.name: MO HealthNet
programs.tagline: Missouri Medicaid that covers healthcare costs and long-term care for parents 65+ with limited income

localEntities.name: Local Department of Social Services Family Support Division offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and has limited income, MO HealthNet can cover their healthcare costs including doctor visits, prescriptions, emergency care, and hospital stays. Because this is Missouri's Medicaid program for seniors, it also covers long-term care — either nursing home care or home-based services to help your parent stay in their own home.

The program has different income limits depending on what level of care your parent needs. For basic healthcare coverage, a single person can earn up to $860/month. For home-based care services, the limit is higher at $1,311/month. Your parent can have up to $3,000 in countable assets ($6,000 for couples).

If your parent's income is slightly over the limit, they may still qualify through a 'spend down' process where medical expenses reduce their countable income to the qualifying level.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services, your parent must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at mydss.mo.gov in 15 minutes, with processing typically taking 45-90 days for long-term care.

steps.title: Complete the MO HealthNet Application online
steps.description: Go to mydss.mo.gov/healthcare/apply and fill out the main application form

steps.title: Submit the Supplemental Form
steps.description: Required for anyone 65+, blind, disabled, or needing long-term care — failure to submit this form can result in denial

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Upload proof of income, assets, identity, and medical records if applying for long-term care services

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment if needed
steps.description: For long-term care, a professional will assess your parent's need for daily assistance with activities

applicationGuide.processingTime: 45-90 days for long-term care applications due to required assessments
applicationGuide.waitlist: Home and community-based waiver services may have waitlists due to limited slots
applicationGuide.tip: Don't skip the Supplemental Form — it's required for all seniors and missing it will delay or deny your application

tiers.name: Basic Coverage (Non-Spend Down)
tiers.description: Healthcare coverage including doctor visits, prescriptions, emergency care, and hospital stays
tiers.coverage: Standard medical care, short-term hospital stays, prescription drugs

tiers.name: Home & Community Based Waiver
tiers.description: Allows your parent to receive long-term care services at home instead of a nursing facility
tiers.coverage: Home health, personal care, adult day services, home modifications, plus all basic coverage

tiers.name: Nursing Home Coverage
tiers.description: Full nursing facility care with resident paying their income toward care costs
tiers.incomeLimit: All income goes toward care costs (no fixed income limit)
tiers.coverage: Complete nursing facility care, medical services, personal needs allowance

tiers.name: Spend Down Option
tiers.description: Qualify by deducting medical expenses from income to reach program limits
tiers.incomeLimit: Reduces to basic limits through medical bills
tiers.coverage: Same as tier qualified for after spend down calculation

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (with equity limits for applicants 55+)

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or Social Security award letters
Medicare or VA benefits information if applicable

categories.name: Medical (for long-term care)
items: Medical records showing need for daily assistance

contentSections.text: Home and community-based waiver services have waitlists due to limited funding slots. Apply early if your parent wants to receive care at home rather than in a facility.
If your parent is 55+ and owns a home, consult an elder law attorney before applying. Home equity limits apply and can affect eligibility, especially for long-term care coverage.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the $860 limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through 'spend down' — their medical expenses each month are subtracted from their income. If the remaining amount is under $860, they qualify for that month.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is typically exempt. However, if your parent is 55+ and applies for long-term care, there are home equity limits that can affect eligibility. Consult an elder law attorney for guidance.

faqs.question: What's the difference between nursing home coverage and home-based care?
faqs.answer: Home-based care (HCBS waiver) has a $1,311/month income limit and lets your parent stay home with services coming to them. Nursing home coverage has no income limit, but your parent pays all their income toward the facility costs, keeping only a small personal needs allowance.

faqs.question: How long does the clinical assessment take for long-term care?
faqs.answer: The Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment adds 45-90 days to processing time. A professional evaluates whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities.

faqs.question: What happens if I forget to submit the Supplemental Form?
faqs.answer: Your application will likely be denied. The Supplemental Form is required for anyone 65+, blind, disabled, or applying from a nursing facility. Submit it with your main application to avoid delays.

faqs.question: Are there waitlists for services?
faqs.answer: Yes, home and community-based waiver services may have waitlists because funding is limited. Basic healthcare coverage and nursing facility care don't have waitlists, but home-based services do in some areas.

programs.name: Structured Family Caregiving Waiver
programs.tagline: If you're already caring for a parent with dementia at home, Missouri may pay you through this program.

localEntities.name: Department of Health and Senior Services, Division of Senior and Disability Services

programs.intro:
If you're already living with and caring for a parent who has Alzheimer's or dementia, Missouri's Structured Family Caregiving Waiver can turn your unpaid caregiving into paid employment. The state pays a caregiving agency, which then employs you to provide the hands-on care you're already giving — bathing, dressing, medication management, and daily support.

This isn't a program to hire new caregivers. Your parent must already live full-time in your household, have a confirmed dementia diagnosis from a Missouri doctor, and qualify for Medicaid. Because the program requires nursing-home-level care needs, your parent must need significant daily help with basic activities like eating, toileting, or getting dressed.

The state designed this as a cost-effective alternative to nursing facility placement, allowing families to keep their loved ones at home while receiving compensation for the intensive care required.
programs.savingsSource: Payment amount not specified in official sources

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care through InterRAI HC assessment — scoring shows need for daily help with activities like mobility, eating, toileting, bathing, or dressing
otherRequirements: Diagnosed with Alzheimer's or related dementia by licensed Missouri physician
Has backup caregiver plan in place
Not living in facility or group home
Not enrolled in other Medicaid waiver programs

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Missouri's Division of Senior and Disability Services for assessment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Confirm Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: Your parent must already have active Medicaid or qualify for it. If on spenddown, liability must be met for services to continue.

steps.title: Get dementia diagnosis confirmed
steps.description: Licensed Missouri physician must diagnose Alzheimer's or related dementia disorder as defined by state law.

steps.title: Contact DSDS for assessment
steps.description: Healthcare professional will evaluate through InterRAI HC assessment to confirm nursing facility level of care needs.

steps.title: Establish backup caregiver plan
steps.description: Identify qualified substitute caregiver who knows your parent's needs and can provide coverage when you're unavailable.

steps.title: Complete enrollment
steps.description: DSDS will coordinate with caregiving agency to employ you as the paid caregiver for your parent's approved care plan.

applicationGuide.tip: Start with confirming Medicaid eligibility first — without active Medicaid, your parent cannot access this waiver program.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over $1,500
excluded: Home if spouse, minor child, or disabled adult child lives there

categories.name: Medical
items: Alzheimer's/dementia diagnosis from licensed Missouri physician
Medical records showing daily care needs

categories.name: Living Situation
items: Backup caregiver plan and contact information

contentSections.text: This waiver expires June 30, 2025, and renewal is pending. New applications may be affected if the program is not renewed.
You must already be living with and caring for your parent before applying — this program authorizes payment for existing caregiving arrangements, not new ones.

faqs.question: Can my spouse with dementia qualify if I'm already their caregiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, spouses can be primary caregivers under this program. Your spouse must meet all other requirements — dementia diagnosis, nursing facility level of care, and Medicaid eligibility. The caregiving agency will employ you to provide the care you're already giving.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is too high for regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: She may qualify through Medicaid spenddown, where she pays a monthly amount toward medical expenses before Medicaid coverage begins. However, waiver services only operate when the spenddown liability is met — otherwise you'd pay privately for that period.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as equity doesn't exceed $730,000. Since she must live full-time with you as her caregiver, the home exemption typically applies whether she owns it or you do.

faqs.question: What happens if I need to travel or take a break from caregiving?
faqs.answer: You must have an established backup plan with a qualified substitute caregiver before enrollment. This person must be chosen by your parent (or guardian), employed by the same provider agency, and familiar with your parent's specific care needs.

faqs.question: How much will I be paid as the family caregiver?
faqs.answer: The official sources don't specify exact payment rates or hours. The caregiving agency receives payment from the state and employs you to provide hands-on services according to your parent's approved plan of care. Contact DSDS for current rate information.

faqs.question: What if Dad has dementia but lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: He cannot qualify while living in any facility, group home, or boarding home. The program requires full-time residence in the same household as the primary caregiver. He would need to move in with you to be eligible.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) - Missouri
programs.tagline: All-inclusive medical care, adult day programs, and transportation with no co-pays for parents who need nursing facility-level care but want to stay home.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing facility-level care but wants to stay at home, PACE provides all medical care, prescription drugs, adult day programs, meals, and transportation through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers everything under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, specialists, or insurance plans — one interdisciplinary team manages all their care.

PACE is only available in three Missouri regions: Southwest Missouri, St. Louis, and Kansas City. Your parent must be 55+ and certified by the state as needing nursing facility-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility). About 90% of PACE participants have both Medicare and Medicaid, which covers all costs. Private-pay participants pay $7,000+ annually in monthly premiums.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Missouri as needing nursing facility level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE service area
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE support
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plans, or hospice

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE organization directly for assessment and enrollment — processing time varies by location.

steps.title: Contact your local PACE organization
steps.description: Call Jordan Valley (Southwest), EverTrue PACE (St. Louis), or PACE KC (Kansas City) based on your location

steps.title: Complete nursing facility level of care assessment
steps.description: State-certified assessor evaluates whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management

steps.title: Attend PACE center visit
steps.description: Your parent visits the PACE center to meet the interdisciplinary team and see the adult day program

steps.title: Enroll if eligible
steps.description: Sign enrollment agreement and transition existing care to PACE team coordination

applicationGuide.tip: Call the PACE organization serving your area first — they handle the entire process including the state care assessment.

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in three Missouri regions. If your parent lives outside Southwest Missouri, St. Louis, or Kansas City areas, they cannot access this program.

PACE itself has no income or asset limits, but if your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid, they'll pay $7,000+ annually in monthly premiums. Apply for Medicaid first if your parent has limited income.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE is designed to help people stay in their homes instead of moving to nursing facilities. The program provides all necessary support services to make this possible.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is too high for Medicaid but he needs PACE?
faqs.answer: Your father can still enroll in PACE as a private-pay participant. He'll pay monthly premiums of $7,000+ annually, but this may still cost less than nursing facility care while allowing him to stay home.

faqs.question: Does PACE replace Mom's current Medicare and doctors?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE becomes your mother's primary health plan and coordinates all care through their interdisciplinary team. She cannot keep Medicare Advantage plans or outside specialists — all care goes through PACE.

faqs.question: What if we live 30 minutes from the nearest PACE center?
faqs.answer: PACE provides transportation to and from the adult day center and all medical appointments. However, you must live within the PACE organization's designated service area to be eligible.

faqs.question: Can Dad leave PACE if he doesn't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE enrollment is voluntary and participants can disenroll at any time. However, he would need to find new Medicare and Medicaid coverage and coordinate his own care team again.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom needs emergency care while in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE covers all emergency care and hospital stays. The PACE team coordinates with emergency services and manages the transition back to community care — no separate insurance claims or authorizations needed.
